title: The Birth of a Revolutionary Era**

lesson.learn: The first computers were massive machines that took up entire rooms due to their complex designs and need for extensive cooling systems. These early computing machines played a crucial role in shaping modern technology, transforming industries, and changing the way people live and work. Their size and complexity limited accessibility, making them available only to large institutions and governments. The development of these initial computers laid the foundation for the modern computer industry.
lesson.simpler: Imagine walking into a giant room filled with massive machines that look like they belong in a science fiction movie. These early computers were so big that they needed their own special building, kind of like a giant air-conditioned box. They made so much noise and heat that only a few people could use them at a time.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The most important thing to remember is that the first computers were massive machines that dominated entire rooms due to their size, complexity, and power requirements.
lesson.deeperDive: One of the earliest examples of such massive computers was ENIAC (Electronic Numerical Integrator And Computer), developed in the late 1940s. This behemoth weighed over 27 tons, stood almost 10 feet tall, and took up an entire room at the University of Pennsylvania's Moore School of Electrical Engineering. As technology advanced, newer computers like UNIVAC I (1951) and IBM System/360 (1964) further reduced size while increasing capabilities. Today, supercomputers are being developed to tackle complex tasks such as climate modeling and medical research. Modern cloud computing has also made it possible for individuals to access powerful computing resources remotely.
lesson.funFact: Did you know that the world's first electronic computer, Colossus, was built in secret during World War II by British engineers?

people: * John Mauchly - Co-inventor of ENIAC and pioneer in developing early computers
* J. Presper Eckert - Co-inventor of ENIAC and renowned computer engineer
* Alan Turing - Mathematician, computer scientist, and logician who made significant contributions to computer science
places: * University of Pennsylvania's Moore School of Electrical Engineering (home to ENIAC)
* Cambridge University's Laboratory for Computer Science (site of early computer developments)
* Bell Labs in New Jersey (where the first electronic switching system was developed)
years: * 1936 - Konrad Zuse invents the Z1, considered the world's first fully automatic digital computer
* 1946 - ENIAC is completed and unveiled to the public
* 1958 - The Integrated Circuit is invented by Jack Kilby
concepts: * Binary Code - A method of representing data using only two digits (0 and 1)
* Vacuum Tubes - Early electronic components used in computers for amplification and switching
* Input/Output Devices - Machines that allow users to interact with the computer, such as keyboards and monitors
